How the JavaScript Engine Works: Understanding What Happens Before Your Code Reaches the CPU
When I first started learning JavaScript, my focus was simply on writing code that worked. I understood variables, loops, functions, and basic syntax, but I had never really thought about what happens after I press Run.
As I continue learning software testing, automation, and JavaScript, I also want to understand how the technology works behind the scenes — not just how to use it.
Recently, I learned how a modern JavaScript engine executes our code, and it completely changed the way I think about JavaScript.
Why Should We Care?
Whether you’re a developer or an aspiring QA Automation Engineer, understanding how JavaScript executes can help you:

• Write more efficient code

• Better understand performance

• Debug issues more effectively

• Appreciate what happens behind the scenes before your code reaches the CPU
The Journey of a JavaScript Program
Before the CPU executes our program, the JavaScript engine performs several important steps.
1. Source Code
Everything starts with the code we write.
let message = "Hello, World!";
console.log(message);This is called source code because it’s the human-readable version of our program.
2. Lexical Analysis (Tokenization)
The JavaScript engine reads the source code and breaks it into smaller pieces called tokens.
For example:
let
message
=
"Hello, World!"
;These tokens are much easier for the engine to understand than raw text.
The parser checks whether the code follows JavaScript’s grammar rules.
If everything is valid, it creates an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
An AST is a tree-like representation of the program that helps the engine understand the logical structure of our code.
4. Ignition Interpreter
In Google’s V8 JavaScript Engine, the AST is converted into bytecode by the Ignition Interpreter.
Bytecode allows the engine to start executing our program quickly without compiling everything into machine code first.
5. Profiling
While the program is running, the engine monitors which parts of the code execute most frequently.
These frequently executed sections are known as hot code.
6. TurboFan (JIT Compiler)
Once hot code is identified, TurboFan (V8’s Just-In-Time Compiler) converts that code into native machine code.
This optimization significantly improves performance.
7. Garbage Collection
Finally, the JavaScript engine automatically removes objects that are no longer reachable by the program.
This process is known as Garbage Collection, and it helps free memory while keeping applications efficient.
The Complete Execution Pipeline
Source Code
↓
Lexical Analysis (Tokenization)
↓
Parsing
↓
Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
↓
Ignition Interpreter
↓
Bytecode
↓
Profiling
↓
TurboFan (JIT Compiler)
↓
Native Machine Code
↓
CPU ExecutionPress enter or click to view image in full size

My Biggest Takeaway
Before learning this topic, I mostly focused on JavaScript syntax.
Now I appreciate how much work the JavaScript engine performs behind the scenes before a single line of code reaches the CPU.
Understanding this execution pipeline gives me a stronger foundation as I continue learning JavaScript, Playwright, and QA Automation.
